title: Setting Fees As A Healer Without Guilt
dek: You swallow your price before it even leaves your mouth, your jaw tight with the inherited belief that healing must be given away. But your resentment is a biological fact, and your body is keeping the ledger.
bodyMarkdown:
## When Saying Your Fee Feels Like Swallowing Screaming


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.237Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You sit with the weight of the moment pressing down on your chest like a storm-heavy sky just before midnight. The room is quiet except for the faint hum of the heater, and your body aches in ways you didn’t expect. Your masseter muscle clenches so tight it throbs behind your cheekbones, a low, pulsing reminder of the words you haven’t yet spoken. The fee you want to state feels like a secret you’re not allowed to share, a forbidden truth tucked away behind a wall of hesitance and half-smiles. Your throat tightens, your diaphragm contracts unevenly - as if your breath itself has been caught in a trap made of old stories about generosity and worth.

You’ve been here before. The moment when you have to say the number, set the boundary of your value in currency, and it twists your insides like a slow churn. The psoas, that deep core muscle that holds your spine in place, tightens reflexively. It’s a visceral knot that doesn’t just live in your belly but radiates, tugging your lower back and hips into a rigid cage. You feel it as a hardened place where your body archives every instance you were told your care was charity, where your labor was ‘just a gift,’ where your worth was measured in how much you gave without asking for much in return.

The guilt arrives like a shadow, a cold hand pressing into your trapezius and levator scapulae, making your shoulders creep up around your ears in a defensive shrug. You’re not just battling numbers; you’re wrestling with decades of cultural programming that says women’s anger - yes, the anger that fuels your insistence on fair pay - is ugly, unwelcome, something to be masked or minimized. So when you finally open your mouth to speak your fee, it feels like swallowing screaming. Your voice wavers because it carries the weight of that suppressed fury, the body’s memory of being dismissed, minimized, or outright ignored.

Undercharging isn’t just a miscalculation; it’s a betrayal of your own biology. It’s the jaw that remembers clenching through meetings where your expertise was questioned, the psoas that tightens in the aftermath of every boundary crossed, the diaphragm that fails to release a full breath because you’re holding something back - not just money, but your full presence, your rightful claim to care for yourself as fiercely as you care for others.

You might catch yourself apologizing, hedging the fee with words that diminish its legitimacy. Maybe the old stories whisper, “They can’t afford it,” “They might not come back,” or “You don’t want to seem greedy.” Each thought etches tension deeper into the fascia around your neck and shoulders, each hesitation a thread pulling your energy away from your center. Your body doesn’t lie; it knows this is not pathology, this is biology. Your muscles, your breath, your very posture are coded with the history of a body that was never allowed to say yes to its own worth without shame.

But here’s the truth your body is trying to remind you of in the middle of that tight, anxious moment: claiming your fee is an act of honoring the work your hands have learned, the intuition your skin carries, the fierce intelligence your nervous system has cultivated through years of listening and healing. This is not about greed or vanity. This is about setting a boundary that your psoas can relax into, a number your masseter can speak without trembling, a stance your shoulders can hold without collapsing into guilt.

You are not just stating a price; you are asserting your right to hold space for your own care, your own survival, your own power. And that is a revolution in every muscle, every cell, every breath.

## The Body Remembers: When Saying No to Your Worth is a Somatic Story

You feel it before your mind catches up - the tightening in your masseter, that subtle clench of your jaw muscles, a silent protest lodged beneath your carefully composed face. The body remembers the old story, the one that says healers like you - those who tend to others’ wounds, who cradle pain and coax life back into tired flesh - should do so without asking for much in return. It’s a story written not in words but in fascia, in the subtle maps of tension threading through your trapezius and levator scapulae, a map you can trace with your fingertips at 3 a.m. in the quiet, when the world is still and your breath shallow and uneven like a hesitant tide.

Gabor Maté speaks to this somatic truth like a medicine man recognizing the patterns etched out by survival itself. When you suppress your own truth to keep a tenuous attachment - whether it’s the fragile approval of a community, the unspoken expectation of family, or the internalized message that charging for healing is somehow "wrong" - your body says no. It says no not in words, but in the tautness of your psoas, the shallow catch of your diaphragm, the way your breath hitches just before you can speak your worth aloud. This is not pathology. This is biology. The body is wired to protect what it needs, and when your authenticity threatens to fracture a connection, your nervous system tightens the perimeter.

You’ve been taught, maybe from the earliest days of your healing practice, that money and healing don’t mix - or if they do, only sparingly, only as a reluctant concession. You might carry the residue of that lesson in the subtle bracing between your shoulder blades, or the way your wrists feel just a little stiff when you reach for the ledger, or the dry, almost metallic taste that rises in your mouth when you speak the words “my fees.” This is the body’s way of saying, “I remember. I remember when you were told your work was charity, your care was grace, and your time should be given freely.” It is the body’s archive of cultural and familial conditioning, a somatic ledger far older than your conscious mind.

But your body also holds the capacity for re-education. The same muscles that rebel against your worth can soften when your nervous system registers safety. When you settle into a warm evening with the scent of chamomile and lavender curling through the air - perhaps brewing a cup of [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) - you invite your body to relax into a new story. The fragrant steam rising from the cup unfurls the tension knotted in your diaphragm, coaxing your breath deeper, coaxing the mind to quiet enough to hear the truth beneath the old narrative.

You are not a contradiction. You are a healer who deserves to be paid, not out of greed or vanity, but out of respect for the labor of your body and spirit. That respect begins with acknowledging the silent dialogues happening in your fascia and muscles, those whispered protests and agreements you carry. When you set your fees, you are not just assigning a number; you are teaching your body a new language of worthiness, a language where boundaries are held in the gentle strength of the levator scapulae rather than clenched in the desperate grip of fear.

The body knows. It remembers the old "shoulds" and "should nots," but it is also the vessel of transformation. With each breath you claim, with each fee you set, you rewrite the story of what it means to be a healer in the world. It’s not about guilt or shame. It’s about honoring the intricate web of muscles, nerves, and memories that make you who you are. And when you do, the body softens. The jaw unclenches. The psoas releases its tight hold. The nervous system whispers, finally, “Yes.”

## The Body Holds the Ledger: Ancestral Trauma and the Fear of Taking Space

You sit with your hands folded in your lap, and beneath the surface, a silent ledger unfurls - a tally of every whispered message about money, worth, and deserving that your body has collected over generations. This is not mere metaphor. The jaw tightens - masseter clenched, a barricade against the words you never said aloud. The diaphragm constricts as if to choke down unspoken truths. These are the muscles that remember the old stories: "You must not ask for too much," "Your value is not counted in coins," "To want is to be greedy." Resmaa Menakem teaches us that trauma is not lodged solely in the mind; it is flesh and fascia, it is the nervous system’s unyielding grip. This is somatic abolitionism - not in the political sense alone, but the breaking down of inherited trauma that insists you shrink yourself to fit into a space too small for your healing.

When you fear setting your fees because it feels like taking up too much room, you are not alone. This discomfort is a whisper from the ancestors, the echoes of scarcity and shame woven tightly into your body’s blueprint. Your levator scapulae might stiffen with each thought of declaring your worth; your trapezius knotted as if bracing for judgment. This is not pathology. This is biology. Your body is responding to a lineage of survival strategies that taught you to minimize, to soften your presence, and to make yourself small so others could feel safe.

In the quiet, when you consider naming your price, you may notice a flutter in your psoas - a muscle that holds not just posture but primal fears about safety and belonging. That flutter is a signal, a conversation between your present self and the ancestors who endured poverty, exploitation, or dismissal. Your body knows this history deeply. It is why you hesitate, why guilt creeps in like a shadow in the early morning fog. But the body also holds the wisdom to rewrite these patterns. It knows that taking up space is not the same as taking from others. Your presence, your voice, your work - all deserve to fill the room fully.

There is no shame in asking for what you are worth. The herbs you use to craft your medicine - the valerian root that calms the restless masseter, the nervy tendrils of chamomile that soothe the trembling psoas - are not free. Your time, your energy, your skill are not free. The ledger in your body must balance, not by minimizing your needs but by honoring them. When you feel the resistance, invite your hands to rest on your ribcage and breathe deeply, sensing the diaphragm’s slow rise and fall. Let your body taste the possibility of safety in claiming space.

If the night is restless, with the weight of ancestral doubt pressing on your chest like the humidity of a summer storm, consider the gentle medicine of [FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20). It works softly on the nervous system, unbinding the tension in the diaphragm and masseter, helping the body to remember that it can relax into its full presence without fear.

You are not just a healer - you are a vessel of lineage and change. Setting your fees without guilt is a radical act of somatic abolitionism, a reclaiming of space your ancestors dreamed you would one day occupy. Your body is not a ledger to be balanced by scarcity but a living testament to abundance and resilience. When you name your price, you name your worth. And your body, muscle by muscle, remembers that it is allowed to take up space.

## The Heat of Righteous Anger: When Value Meets Resistance

It’s a late Wednesday afternoon. The air is thick with humidity, the kind of heat that doesn’t just sit on your skin but seeps deep into your bones. You’ve just wrapped a session, and there it is: the client’s hesitant, almost apologetic question about your fee. Or maybe it’s the sinking realization that the rent notice is staring at you from the kitchen counter, its weight pressing down harder than any muscle knot. This is the moment your body lights a fire - hot, sharp, undeniable - that is not shame, not embarrassment, but pure, righteous anger.

You feel it first in the diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle sitting just beneath your lungs, the one that contracts with each breath. It tightens, almost spasming, like it’s trying to push something out that’s been lodged too long. The breath catches in your throat, shallow and quick, a warning flare that the body’s not about to swallow this slight quietly. Simultaneously, your levator scapulae - the muscles running along the back and side of your neck, anchoring your shoulder blades - bunch up with a fierce tension. They pull your shoulders upward, a reflexive shield, bracing you against the invisible weight of judgement. You can almost feel the fascia between those muscles thickening, resisting the pressure of devaluation.

This anger is not a flaw. It is your body’s way of saying, “No more.” It is the exact same fire that has clenched your masseter muscles when you’ve been told your worth is less than the price on your sign. The jaw remembers every time you’ve swallowed your fury, every time you’ve adjusted your fee downward to avoid discomfort - only to feel that same tightening coil of resentment twist deeper. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body is wired to protect the value of your work, your time, your medicine.

When a client questions your fee, it can feel like an assault, but it’s also an invitation. This is where your integrity meets their assumptions. You can feel the anger ripple through your trapezius muscles, those broad bands stretching from your neck down to your mid-back, as if you’re bracing yourself to stand taller, to claim your space. It’s a muscle memory of self-defense, a somatic shout: “I am worth this.” The heat pulses there, raw and electric, reminding you that you’re not being greedy or selfish. You are honoring your craft, your hours, the deep well of knowledge and experience that underpins every session.

But the anger isn’t just for the client. It’s also the frustration that wells up when the bills pile higher than the money in your account. When you can’t pay your own rent, the same muscles tighten, but the sensation shifts - the anger turns inward, a bitter knot in your solar plexus that drags your diaphragm down instead of lifting it. You feel the levator scapulae pull, but this time it’s less defensive, more exhausted. The body is tired of begging, of shrinking to fit someone else’s budget or expectations. It’s a physical ache that calls for respect, for boundaries that protect you from depletion.

In these moments, the body craves care that matches the intensity of the emotions. You might reach for something like [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20), warming the back of your neck and shoulders, easing the tight levator scapulae and trapezius as the oil’s herbal potency seeps into the fascia. The scent of arnica, earthy and pungent, grounds you, reminding you that this is a muscle and tissue issue, not a moral failing. The oil helps unravel those dense knots of anger that have no place being locked inside your body.

This heat, this fury, is not your enemy. It is your body’s way of marking boundaries, demanding fairness, and insisting on respect. It’s a signal that your work is real, your time is precious, and your presence is medicine. When you feel that tightening in your diaphragm and levator scapulae, know that you’re not alone. The body knows its worth, even when the world tries to tell you otherwise.

So when the question about your fee comes, or when the rent notice threatens your peace, lean into the heat. Let your body’s anger be your guide. It’s not about guilt, it’s about justice - to yourself first, then to those who seek your healing hands.

## Grounding Your Nervous System Before Stating Your Fee

You stand with your feet bare, toes spreading just enough to meet the cool floor beneath you. It’s late afternoon, that liminal hour when the sun begins to soften, and the air carries a faint scent of jasmine and damp earth through the open window. Your heels press into the floor, the weight of your body sinking into the fascia of your arches, the subtle stretch of the Achilles tendon reminding you that you are anchored. This is where the work begins - not in the mind, but in the physical reality of your stance. Before you state your fee, before you open your mouth to claim your worth, you must first convince your nervous system that it is safe to do so.

Begin by bringing your awareness to your pelvis. Notice if it’s tucked under in a defensive posture or tilted forward in an eager, people-pleasing stance. Gently rock it until you find the center, the place where your psoas muscle doesn’t have to grip to hold you upright. Let your knees soften, just a fraction of an inch, unlocking the rigid bracing that often accompanies the fear of confrontation. You can feel the shift travel up your spine, a subtle unwinding of the tension that gathers in the lower back when you anticipate rejection.

Now, place one hand on your belly, just below the navel, and the other on your chest, over the sternum. Close your eyes if it feels safe, or let your gaze soften on a spot a few feet ahead of you. Breathe in through your nose, directing the air down into the hand on your belly. Feel the diaphragm expanding, the lumbar fascia stretching subtly against the psoas muscle, which quietly lengthens in the background. You count to four silently as you inhale, feeling the breath infiltrate every cell, the tissue softening around your kidneys and spine.

Your exhale is longer, slower, releasing through parted lips with a slight hum, a sound that vibrates softly from your diaphragm up through the sternum, loosening the levator scapulae and trapezius muscles that often clutch tight when you brace yourself for confrontation. The hum is not effortful - it’s a release, a signal to your nervous system that tension can dissolve.

You repeat this cycle, inhaling for four counts, exhaling for six, grounding deeper with every breath. Your jaw, the masseter muscle that clenches when you anticipate discomfort, unclenches. The corners of your mouth soften, and your tongue settles against the roof of your mouth, creating a subtle lift that encourages openness.

With your body aligned, your nervous system soothed, you feel the power in your stance. You are neither shrinking nor sprawling; you are present, embodied, and unashamed. The weight you carry is not just physical - it’s the sum of your knowledge, your experience, your commitment. This grounding practice reminds you that stating your fee is not an act of greed or guilt but an expression of your worth and your dedication to the craft.

When the moment comes to say the number, you do so with the steady authority of muscles that know safety and boundaries. You have rooted yourself in the earth, breathed into your body’s wisdom, and allowed your jaw to remember the truth: your healing work holds value, and your fee honors that.

## FAQ

**Q:** How do I stop my chest from tightening every time I think about charging what I’m worth?

**A:** That tightness, often nestled just beneath your sternum where the pectoralis major hooks close to the ribs, is your body’s way of signaling a conflict. It’s not just a feeling - it’s a muscle and fascia reaction to old stories you carry about worthiness and care. Your chest tightens because you’re squeezing something down, maybe the truth that your labor and your knowledge have value. This isn’t pathology; it’s biology. When you notice that, try a slow, deliberate breath into that space, letting your diaphragm fill and soften the area. Say to yourself, “My work is medicine. My time is medicine.” Over time, the body remembers that this tightness doesn’t need to hold space for guilt. It’s safe to let it go.

**Q:** Why do my jaw and neck get so tense when I talk about money with clients?

**A:** The masseter and levator scapulae muscles are often the first to clench when you engage in conversations about fees, especially if you were taught that talking money is somehow icky or aggressive. That clenching is your body’s gatekeeper trying to protect you from vulnerability. The levator scapulae, which runs from your neck vertebrae to the scapula, stiffens to brace your whole posture, while the masseter contracts with a force that can make your temples throb. This is not a failing; your body is doing exactly what it’s been trained to do - to hold tension when you feel exposed or judged. To soften this, try a ritual before these conversations: gently massage your jaw muscles, hum low and steady to relax the vagus nerve, and remind yourself, “I am a conduit for healing, not a salesperson.” Your body will start to lower the defenses as you rewrite the narrative you tell it.

**Q:** I feel guilty charging for sessions because I want to help everyone. How do I hold that compassion without self-sacrifice?

**A:** Compassion is not depletion. When your trapezius muscles carry the weight of everyone’s needs, dragging your shoulders toward your ears, your body is screaming for boundaries. The trapezius is a big muscle, but it’s sensitive to emotional overload. Your compassion is a resource, not an unlimited faucet. When you set fees, you are not turning away the people who need you; you are creating a container that keeps you whole. The body knows this. It knows when you are overextending because your psoas tightens, your pelvis shifts, and your breath becomes shallow. Honoring your boundaries with clear fees is an act of deep care for yourself and those you serve. This is not guilt, it’s biology recalibrating.

**Q:** What can I do when old stories about money and worthiness flood me during a session?

**A:** These stories often lodge in your nervous system like a stuck note - your heart races, your diaphragm clamps down, and your forehead wrinkles with tension. The body remembers every time you were told your worth was conditional or your fury was ugly. When this happens during a session, ground yourself through your body. Feel your feet on the floor, notice the subtle engagement of your tibialis anterior muscle stabilizing your stance. Place one hand gently on your solar plexus and the other on your belly, inviting your breath into that space. Whisper internally, “I am here. This is medicine. My value is not a question.” Let the muscles around your ribcage soften. These are not just affirmations - they are somatic arrests of old fear, rewiring your body’s relationship with money and worth. This is fierce healing, unflinching and tender all at once.
